icit_database.name: ICIT (Interactive Corpus of Indus Texts)
icit_database.access: Request from Dr. Fuls by email — he's our Phase-109 contact
icit_database.priority: HIGH — Dr. Fuls is our primary outreach target
icit_database.crosswalk_needed: Wells 3-digit ↔ Mahadevan M-number (partially done in Phase-51/71)

failaka_seals.name: Failaka Island seals (Kuwait)
failaka_seals.relevance: Dilmun-type seals with Indus inscriptions; maritime trade hub
failaka_seals.fish_sign_relevance: HIGH — coastal/maritime context exactly where isolated fish commodity marks would appear
failaka_seals.access: Published in Kjaerum 1983, Tell F6 in Hojlund 2012 (both available Academia.edu)

saar_seals.name: Saar settlement seals (Bahrain)
saar_seals.relevance: Largest single Bronze Age seal corpus from Bahrain; shows inland vs coastal distribution
saar_seals.fish_sign_relevance: MEDIUM — Saar is inland Dilmun, not coastal port
saar_seals.access: Available as free PDF on Academia.edu

janabiyah_seal.name: Janabiyah contact-zone seal (Bahrain Gulf coast)
janabiyah_seal.source: Already in our corpus — Phase-46 analysis
janabiyah_seal.relevance: Has all 7 HIGH anchor signs — key contact-zone artifact

cisi_vol3_gulf.name: CISI Vol 3 Gulf material (Parpola)
cisi_vol3_gulf.source: Already partially ingested via Phase-28 OCR pipeline
cisi_vol3_gulf.relevance: Contains Gulf assemblage seals in Parpola P-numbering
cisi_vol3_gulf.status: PARTIALLY DONE — Phase-28 OCR extracted some data

step_1_icit_request.action: Email Dr. Fuls for ICIT data access
step_1_icit_request.template:
Dear Dr. Fuls,

Following our earlier correspondence (Phase-109 package, May 2026), we are now specifically working on the fish-sign polysemy question raised by Avishai Roif (Ben Gurion University). In the Holdat corpus, both M047 and M001 appear exclusively in compound sequences — no isolated occurrences. This suggests the commodity-tally usage predicted by the polysemy model would appear in Gulf assemblages (Failaka, Saar) rather than formal seals.

Would it be possible to obtain access to the ICIT database, specifically for the Gulf-site inscriptions? We are particularly interested in:
1. Failaka and Saar seal data (sign sequences + site attribution)
2. Any isolated single-sign inscriptions at coastal Gulf sites
3. Fish-sign (Wells code for M047/M001) occurrence data by site

We would be happy to share our current decipherment results (95.7% token coverage, 263 H+M anchors) in exchange.

Best regards,
Tristen Pierson
Glossa Lab
step_1_icit_request.priority: CRITICAL — this is the fastest path to Gulf corpus data

step_2_failaka_pdfs.action: Download Kjaerum 1983 and Tell F6 2012 from Academia.edu
step_2_failaka_pdfs.then: OCR sign sequences using Phase-104 Mistral pipeline

step_3_saar_pdf.action: Download Crawford 1997 Saar seals from Academia.edu
step_3_saar_pdf.then: Extract sign sequences using Mistral OCR

step_4_crosswalk.action: Map Wells 3-digit codes to Mahadevan M-numbers
step_4_crosswalk.status: Phase-51/71 did 45/390 entries. ICIT uses Wells codes.

step_5_integration.action: Integrate Gulf corpus into Holdat-compatible format
step_5_integration.enables: Fish-sign isolated vs compound test with Gulf assemblages

critical_insight: ICIT is maintained by Dr. Andreas Fuls — our primary outreach contact. The fastest path to Gulf corpus data is to request ICIT access from him directly, citing the fish-sign polysemy question and our existing research relationship. His email is fuls@epigraphica.de (epigraphica.de/indus/).
avishai_connection: The Failaka seals are exactly where Avishai's isolated-fish-commodity hypothesis would be testable. Failaka was a maritime trading hub (2000 BCE Dilmun) — the kind of site where short administrative records on perishable media (now lost) would have existed alongside the circular stamp seals that survived.
user_action_needed: Email Dr. Fuls: fuls@epigraphica.de — request ICIT Gulf data access
Download Avishai's other papers: Academia.edu 129921039 and 129046130
